figures.gloss: The patron deity of Susa and the supreme judge of the dead. He is the ultimate guarantor of oaths, reigning over the deep earth where he intimately weighs the souls of the deceased in the shadowy afterlife.
figures.facet: He seamlessly merges civic and chthonic authority. He is not a distant, exiled lord of the dead, but the living city's active political protector, binding Susa's daily commerce inextricably to underworld justice.
figures.provenance: Middle Elamite royal inscriptions, Susa funerary texts.

figures.gloss: The 'Great Goddess' originally from the coastal region of Liyan. She became the supreme mother of the Elamite pantheon, fiercely associated with fertility, the ocean's depths, and the towering Zagros mountains.
figures.facet: She ruled alongside, and often functionally eclipsed, male supreme gods as an unassailable, independent sovereign. She embodies a matriarchal persistence that heavily resisted the patriarchal syncretism of neighboring Mesopotamia.
figures.provenance: Inscriptions of Untash-Napirisha at Chogha Zanbil, Elamite rock reliefs.

figures.gloss: Literally 'The Great God', the patron of the Anshan highlands. He is an anthropomorphic deity of raw, terrestrial power, often depicted holding a flowing vase of life-giving water while seated upon a coiled, human-headed serpent.
figures.facet: He entirely lacks a personal name—'Napirisha' is merely a title. He embodies the anonymous, untamable majesty of the Zagros mountains, relying on the primordial serpent as his literal throne.
figures.provenance: Kurangun rock reliefs, Chogha Zanbil dedicatory bricks.

figures.gloss: A supreme sky-god of the Elamite highlands, frequently invoked by royalty to legitimize their rule. He commands the celestial order, breathes life into kingship, and watches over the macrocosmic fate of the empire.
figures.facet: He possesses an overwhelming, radiating majesty that he explicitly bestows upon human rulers. His divine presence operates as a highly communicable political weapon, legitimizing Elamite kings against external empires.
figures.provenance: Achaemenid Elamite texts, Neo-Elamite royal names.

figures.gloss: The sun god and the absolute arbiter of justice. He illuminates the day, exposes hidden falsehoods, and ensures that both commerce and royal decrees align flawlessly with cosmic equity.
figures.facet: Elamite legal texts describe him actively 'investing' the marketplace and the scales of merchants. The sun is not just a heavenly observer, but the literal, physical presence of commercial fairness on earth.
figures.provenance: Susa legal tablets, treaties with Naram-Sin of Akkad.

figures.gloss: The divine protection or magical charisma emanating directly from the gods. It is an active, supernatural force that sanctuaries physically house and kings aggressively wield to consecrate their reign.
figures.facet: It operates almost like a physical, localized force field. To 'touch the kidin' was a literal legal and physical act of taking sanctuary or swearing a binding, life-or-death oath.
figures.provenance: Middle Elamite legal texts, administrative records of Susa.

figures.gloss: The monumental ziggurat complex at Dur Untash (Chogha Zanbil). It serves as the physical and spiritual junction between the ancient deities of the Zagros mountains and the gods of the Susiana plain.
figures.facet: Unlike Mesopotamian ziggurats built by stacking smaller terraces on top of larger ones, its inner core was built entirely first, with outer layers wrapped around it—structurally encasing a hidden, sacred center.
figures.provenance: Archaeological excavations at Chogha Zanbil by Roman Ghirshman.

figures.gloss: A profound, recurring motif in Elamite iconography. This immense snake writhes around the thrones of the high gods, serving simultaneously as a water-bearer and an anchor to the chthonic depths.
figures.facet: It is never a monster to be slain. It seamlessly fuses the life-giving flow of mountain springs with the terrifying aspect of the underworld, serving as the living foundation of divine authority.
figures.provenance: Middle Elamite cylinder seals, Kurangun rock reliefs.

figures.gloss: Astral mother-goddess of the early Elamite (Awan/Simash) sphere, equated with Mesopotamian Ishtar in treaty god-lists; the one Elamite deity known to have been exported outward, adopted into Hurrian and then Hittite ritual as an 'Elamite' import goddess.
figures.facet: Unlike Kiririsha, who absorbed her office at home, Pinikir traveled — her cult moved outward into Anatolia even as she faded in her own homeland, a rare case of an Elamite export outliving the exporting culture's memory of her.
figures.provenance: The Suppiluliuma-Sattiwaza treaty god-list (Hittite); Hurro-Hittite Kizzuwatna ritual texts invoking 'Pirinkir.'

figures.gloss: 'God of Elam' and divine herald, named in Mesopotamian and Elamite god-lists alongside Nergal and identified with the planet Mars — a national tutelary figure invoked as protector of the land and its royal house.
figures.facet: He is less a personality than an office: the herald-god's role is defined entirely by relation — messenger, war-god's counterpart, protector — with almost no independent narrative of his own in the surviving corpus.
figures.provenance: Mesopotamian god-lists (An = Anum tradition) equating Simut with Nergal; Elamite royal inscriptions naming him 'god of Elam.'

figures.gloss: 'No-Mercy' — an underworld deity attested at Susa and in Middle Elamite legal-religious texts, who with Ishme-karab confronts the soul at judgment under Inshushinak's presidency; also invoked in Babylonian sources as a stern judge-god.
figures.facet: His epithet does the theological work: naming the judgment's severity so that a companion god, Ishme-karab, can supply the mercy in the very same scene — verdict and appeal split into two separate powers.
figures.provenance: Susa funerary and legal texts; Middle Elamite treaty curse-formulae invoking Lagamal; Neo-Babylonian god-lists.

figures.gloss: 'She-Heard-the-Prayer' — the advocate deity of the Susian dead, attested alongside Lagamal and Inshushinak in funerary and legal texts as the merciful counterweight who intercedes for the soul at judgment.
figures.facet: Where Lagamal names the sentence, Ishme-karab names the appeal — the Susian afterlife stages mercy and severity as two distinct, addressable gods rather than two moods of a single judge.
figures.provenance: Susa funerary texts; Middle Elamite treaty and legal formulae pairing her with Lagamal.

figures.gloss: The rainbow personified as goddess, worshipped in both Elamite and Akkadian religion; honored with a Middle Elamite sanctuary built at Susa under Untash-Napirisha, the same king who raised Kiririsha's temples.
figures.facet: She is one of the clearest points of straightforward Elamite-Mesopotamian religious overlap — not a syncretism of two separate goddesses but a shared deity worshipped under one name by both cultures.
figures.provenance: Untash-Napirisha's dedicatory inscriptions at Susa; Akkadian and Elamite god-lists naming Manzat.

figures.gloss: A 12th-century BCE bronze tableau from Susa, its Elamite name meaning 'sunrise' — a model showing two nude male officiants performing a dawn libation rite among miniature basins, standing stones, and a small ziggurat.
figures.facet: It is the single surviving three-dimensional record of an actual Elamite rite in progress — where nearly every other Elamite religious practice must be reconstructed from inscriptions alone, the Sit-Shamshi freezes the choreography itself in metal.
figures.provenance: Excavated at Susa by the French archaeological mission under Roman Ghirshman; held at the Louvre (Sb 2743).

edges.note: The dual judges: Nahhunte witnesses oaths in the sun, while Inshushinak enforces them in the underworld.
The supreme highland dyad of Anshan and Liyan, frequently invoked together.
The ziggurat was explicitly dedicated to Inshushinak alongside Napirisha.
GOLD SEAM: Kiririsha and Pinikir were explicitly equated with the Mesopotamian Ishtar/Inanna in cross-cultural treaties.
GOLD SEAM: Humban was formally equated with the Mesopotamian sky-father Anu in ancient god lists.
GOLD SEAM: Both rule the dark, inescapable underworld beneath the Mesopotamian-Elamite cultural sphere.
GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme lords of the afterlife who personally weigh and judge the souls of the dead.
GOLD SEAM: Absolute lords of death and uncompromising arbiters of final justice.
GOLD SEAM: Both are solar deities whose all-seeing light makes them the ultimate guarantors of covenants and oaths in greater Iran.
GOLD SEAM: The solar orb conceived not just as light, but as the physical manifestation of cosmic justice and order.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent a manipulable, quasi-physical substance of divine majesty that legitimizes earthly rule.
GOLD SEAM: An invisible, pervasive supernatural force that can be housed in objects or wielded by the spiritually potent.
GOLD SEAM: Architectural and cosmological mountains serving as the supreme axis connecting heaven and earth.
GOLD SEAM: Primal, water-giving serpents that serve as the fundamental, life-bestowing anchors of the world.
GOLD SEAM: Cosmic serpents whose coiled bodies structurally support and bind the divine order.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign earth-mothers inextricably bound to the physical landscape and fertility of their people.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme, over-arching sky who bestows the mandate of kingship upon earthly rulers.
The judgment duo of Susa — severity and mercy split into two addressable gods in the same funerary and legal texts.
Assessor and presiding judge — Lagamal and Ishme-karab confront the soul under Inshushinak's authority.
The advocate operating within the same judgment scene Inshushinak presides over.
Both received Middle Elamite temples under the same royal building program of Untash-Napirisha at Susa.
The two great Elamite goddesses whose treaty-equations with Ishtar ran in parallel — Pinikir exported abroad, Kiririsha ascendant at home.

In Hurro-Hittite Kizzuwatna ritual texts the imported 'Pirinkir' overlaps with Shaushka, the native Hurrian Ishtar-equivalent — two astral love/war goddesses meeting in shared cult practice, not a formal identification.
Treaty god-lists equate Pinikir directly with Ishtar/Inanna, alongside the corpus's existing Kiririsha-Inanna seam.
Nahhunte, the Elamite sun god of justice, is the direct regional counterpart of Utu/Shamash, in the same pattern as the corpus's other documented Mesopotamian-Elamite god-list equations.

dossier.essence: The religion of ancient Elam, the civilization of southwestern Iran centered on Susa in the lowlands and Anshan in the highlands, attested from the third millennium BCE until Elam's absorption into the Achaemenid Persian empire in the mid-first millennium BCE. It was polytheistic, notable for the enduring prominence of great goddesses and for Inshushinak, patron of Susa and judge of the dead.
dossier.cosmos: No Elamite cosmogony or systematic afterlife doctrine survives; the pantheon is reconstructed from names, dedications, and images. At its height it was headed by the highland 'Great God' Napirisha and 'Great Goddess' Kiririsha of Liyan, the sky-god Humban (source of the protective royal radiance, kiten, that legitimized kings), the sun-god and oath-guarantor Nahhunte, and Inshushinak, lord of Susa and of the underworld where the dead were judged. A recurring human-headed serpent bearing life-giving water anchors much of the surviving iconography.
dossier.practice: City temple-cults with royal patronage, oath-taking under divine witness, votive dedication, and monumental building — above all the great ziggurat complex at Chogha Zanbil (Dur-Untash), raised by Untash-Napirisha c. 1250 BCE. Kings advertised their piety and their divine mandate in inscriptions; the material record shows processions, offerings, and seal-borne devotion, though the inner life of the cult is largely lost.
dossier.canon: No sacred literature survives in Elamite; the religion is known only from royal inscriptions (Elamite and Akkadian), theophoric names, god-lists, the god-invoking treaty of Naram-Sin of Akkad (c. 2250 BCE), rock reliefs (Kurangun, Naqsh-e Rostam), cylinder seals, and excavation (Susa, Chogha Zanbil).
dossier.reflects: The persistence of a goddess-weighted, underworld-anchored pantheon on Mesopotamia's eastern flank — a religion recoverable almost entirely from its material and administrative traces rather than from any story it told about itself.
sources: Daniel T. Potts, 'The Archaeology of Elam: Formation and Transformation of an Ancient Iranian State' (2nd ed. 2016)
Wouter F. M. Henkelman, 'The Other Gods Who Are: Studies in Elamite-Iranian Acculturation' (2008)
Javier Álvarez-Mon, Gian Pietro Basello & Yasmina Wicks (eds.), 'The Elamite World' (2018)
